title: Reclaimed-Wood-Look Bedside Table with Three Drawers
summary: A planked-panel nightstand with THREE drawers on euro slides — a rare drawer count for a bedside piece, so squareness during the panel and tie-in steps isn't optional.
description:
This nightstand gets a reclaimed-wood look from planked 1x4 side panels — four boards edge to edge, trimmed flush top and bottom — but the real technical story is that it holds three separate drawers, more than almost any other nightstand in this catalog. That drawer count raises the stakes on a warning the source states directly: whenever a project holds drawers, it is CRITICAL that the whole case is square, or the drawers won't slide correctly. With three drawer openings instead of one, there are three separate chances for a small square-up error to show up as a sticking drawer.

Build the side panels first, pocket-holed together into a solid unit if you have a jig, then trimmed flush top and bottom — check they're perfectly square before moving on, and if a panel isn't coming out flat, add extra 1x2 bracing lined up with the front tie-ins rather than forcing it. Attach the legs to the panel ends flush to the top, then the front and back tie-ins, checking square by measuring from opposite leg to opposite leg on both the top and bottom of the legs — not just once, at both levels.

Each drawer box is built 1" narrower than its housing opening for slide clearance, and the drawer faces go on LAST, positioned with an even 1/8" gap on all sides before nailing — building and testing the drawer boxes' slide action before ever committing to face placement is what keeps the three drawer fronts reading as a clean, evenly-gapped row instead of an obviously hand-fit patchwork.

materials.name: 1/4" plywood, for back and drawer bottoms

cutList.material: 1/4" plywood, for back and drawer bottoms
1/4" plywood, for back and drawer bottoms

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body: Cut everything first. From the 1x4, cut eight side panel boards 23 1/4" long and four top middle boards 21". From the 1x2, cut four side trims 14" and the top back edge 21". From the 2x2, cut four legs 27 1/4", six front and back tie-ins 16", and one centre tie-in 14". From the 1x3, cut the top front edge 21", six drawer sides 14 1/2", and six drawer fronts and backs 15"; from the 1x6, cut three drawer faces 15 3/4". The panel arithmetic checks itself: four 3 1/2" boards side by side make a 14" panel, which is exactly the length of the 14" trim that caps it. Leave the 1/4" plywood back and the three drawer bottoms until the case is built and you can measure them.

steps.title: Build the side panels
steps.body: If using a pocket-hole jig, attach the panel boards to each other first with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws (jig set for 3/4" stock) to make a solid panel. Attach the trim flush to the top and bottom with 1-1/4" finish nails and wood glue. Check that the panels are perfectly square — this matters more here than usual, since the case will hold three drawers. If a panel isn't coming out flat, add extra 1x2 bracing lined up with the front tie-ins.

steps.title: Attach the legs
steps.body: Stand a 27 1/4" leg against each end of a finished side panel, flush with the panel's top and with its outer face, then predrill and drive screws with glue through the leg into the panel's end grain and into the ends of the 14" trims. The panel is 14" wide and each leg is 1 1/2", so a finished side assembly measures 17" across; check both sides come out the same before going further. Keep each leg's two best faces outward, because a nightstand standing away from the wall shows two faces of every leg.

steps.title: Add the tie-ins and check square
steps.body: Attach the front and back tie-ins with predrilled 2-1/2" screws and wood glue. Check the entire project for square by measuring from opposite leg to opposite leg, both at the top and bottom of the legs — with three drawer openings ahead, this step is not optional.

steps.title: Add the center support
steps.body: Fit the 14" centre tie-in between the drawer bays, cut to the same 14" as the side panels' width. Fasten it with predrilled screws and glue into the tie-ins or legs it meets, and check it is level and square to the case front before the glue sets, because the drawer above it and the one below both register off it. The source does not say which way it runs, so dry-fit it against your own drawer layout first. With three drawers stacked, this single piece sets two of the three openings at once.

steps.title: Build the top
steps.body: Build the top face down on the bench, starting at the back with the 21" 1x2 and working forward: the 1x2, then the four 21" 1x4 middle boards, then the 21" 1x3 at the front. That stack is 1 1/2" plus four 3 1/2"s plus 2 1/2", or 18" deep, over a case 17" deep, so the top sits flush at the back and overhangs 1" at the front; its 21" length over the 19" case gives 1" at each side, which matches the overhangs the plan calls for. Join the boards edge to edge with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws and glue, keeping every face flush across the seams. Attach the finished top from underneath so no fastener shows on the surface.

steps.title: Build the drawer boxes
steps.body: Build each drawer box 1" narrower than its housing opening for slide clearance. Attach the plywood bottoms. Install the drawer boxes inset 3/4" from the front of the project and confirm each one slides smoothly before moving on.
materials: 1/4" plywood, for back and drawer bottoms

steps.title: Add the back and drawer faces
steps.body: Attach the plywood back. Position each drawer face over its drawer box with an even 1/8" gap on all sides, then nail on — avoiding hardware locations. Add pulls, sand, and finish.
materials: 1/4" plywood, for back and drawer bottoms

images.alt: Reclaimed-wood-look bedside table with three drawers on euro slides
